events.title: Wahhabi-Saudi Alliance Established at Dir'iyah
events.detail: Muhammad ibn Saud and religious reformer Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab sign a mutual oath (bay'ah) in Dir'iyah, creating the First Saudi State. Under their pact, Ibn Saud wielded political and military authority while Ibn Abd al-Wahhab provided religious legitimacy. The alliance was sealed through intermarriage between their families and created the enduring Wahhabi-Saudi compact that defines the Kingdom to this day.

events.title: Saudi-Wahhabi Forces Launch Regional Expansion
events.detail: The Saudi-Wahhabi emirate begins military expansion from Dir'iyah, asserting control over surrounding Nejd tribes. Ibn Abd al-Wahhab declared jihad against communities practicing Sufi customs or shrine veneration. By 1765 much of central Nejd had been incorporated. This early expansionism planted the ideological seeds of what critics would later call Wahhabi export.

events.title: First Saudi State Captures Riyadh
events.detail: The expanding First Saudi State captures Riyadh, establishing control over central Arabia's most strategically important city. This marked a major milestone in the Saudi consolidation of Nejd. Riyadh would later serve as the capital of the modern Kingdom.

events.title: Wahhabi Forces Sack the Shia Holy City of Karbala
events.detail: A Wahhabi force attacks and sacks Karbala (present-day Iraq), killing hundreds and looting the shrine of Hussein ibn Ali. The attack was motivated by Wahhabi doctrine condemning shrine veneration as polytheism. The incident became a defining grievance in Sunni-Shia relations and in Iranian and Iraqi memory of Saudi Arabia's religious tradition.

events.title: First Saudi State Controls Mecca and Medina
events.detail: The First Saudi State extends control over the Hejaz, including the holy cities of Mecca and Medina, expelling Ottoman administrators. Ibn Abd al-Wahhab's doctrine was enforced: shrines demolished, Sufi practices suppressed. Ottoman Sultan Selim III declared this an unacceptable usurpation and ordered retaliation by sending Egyptian forces under Ibrahim Pasha.

events.title: Egyptian-Ottoman Forces Destroy Dir'iyah
events.detail: After a six-month siege, Ibrahim Pasha's Egyptian-Ottoman forces capture Dir'iyah in September 1818. Saudi Emir Abdullah ibn Saud surrenders, is sent to Constantinople, and publicly beheaded. Dir'iyah is razed, its date palms cut down, and population expelled. The First Saudi State collapses, but survivors regroup within six years to establish the Second Saudi State.

events.title: Second Saudi State Re-established in Riyadh
events.detail: Turki ibn Saud, a cousin of the executed last emir, re-establishes Saudi authority from Riyadh in 1824. The state maintained the Wahhabi religious alliance but operated on a reduced scale, constrained by Egyptian hegemony and internal tribal rivalries. Riyadh replaced the destroyed Dir'iyah as the new Saudi political center.

events.title: Faisal ibn Turki's Reign: Second Saudi State's Golden Age
events.detail: Faisal ibn Turki becomes Saudi ruler for the second time in 1843 after escaping Egyptian captivity. His reign (1843–1865) is considered the most stable period of the Second Saudi State, extending authority across central Arabia and portions of the Persian Gulf coast. Wahhabi governance was systematized. His death in 1865 triggered a devastating succession civil war.

sources.name: Wikipedia — Faisal ibn Turki Al Saud

events.title: Succession War Tears Apart the Second Saudi State
events.detail: The death of Faisal ibn Turki in 1865 triggers a fierce succession struggle between his sons Abdullah and Saud. Their prolonged rivalry allowed the rival Rashidi dynasty of Ha'il to grow in power and begin absorbing Saudi territory. The civil war lasted decades and brought the Second Saudi State to the brink of extinction.

events.title: Rashidis Crush Saudis; Royal Family Exiled to Kuwait
events.detail: In 1891, Muhammad ibn Rashid decisively defeats the last Saudi forces at the Battle of Mulayda. The Al Saud family is forced into exile in Kuwait as guests of the Al-Sabah rulers. Young Abd al-Aziz ibn Saud — later known as Ibn Saud — accompanies his family into exile, absorbing lessons that would fuel his dramatic 1902 reconquest.

events.title: Ibn Saud Recaptures Riyadh in Daring Night Raid
events.detail: On the night of January 15, 1902, 21-year-old Abd al-Aziz ibn Saud leads 40 men over Riyadh's walls, kills the Rashidi governor Ajlan at Masmak Fort, and seizes the city. The brazen raid marks the beginning of 30 years of reconquest. Within weeks, Riyadh and the surrounding area are under Saudi control — the genesis of what becomes the modern Kingdom.

events.title: Ibn Saud Creates the Ikhwan Brotherhood
events.detail: Ibn Saud founds the Ikhwan, a Wahhabi military force settled in agricultural colonies (hijar). Combining military power with religious fervor, the Ikhwan became the shock troops of Ibn Saud's conquests. However, their extremist zeal — including raids into Iraq — eventually made them a liability, and Ibn Saud crushed them militarily at the Battle of Sabilla in 1929.

events.title: Saudi Forces Capture Al-Ahsa from the Ottomans
events.detail: Ibn Saud captures the Al-Ahsa region (present-day Eastern Province) from the weakening Ottoman Empire in 1913. This acquisition proved pivotal: Al-Ahsa sits atop the world's largest oil deposits. British recognition via the Anglo-Saudi Treaty of December 1915 gave Ibn Saud external legitimacy and a monthly stipend in exchange for an anti-Ottoman alliance during WWI.

events.title: Ibn Saud Captures Mecca and the Hejaz
events.detail: After defeating the Hashemite Sheriff of Mecca Hussein ibn Ali, Ibn Saud's forces enter Mecca in October 1924, take Medina in December, and capture Jeddah in December 1925. The conquest of the Hejaz — including Islam's two holiest cities — shocked the Islamic world and gave Ibn Saud the role of custodian of the Two Holy Mosques, a title the Kingdom carries to this day.

events.title: Kingdom of Saudi Arabia Officially Founded
events.detail: On September 23, 1932, Abd al-Aziz ibn Saud issues a royal decree unifying the Sultanate of Nejd and the Kingdom of Hejaz as the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. The name reflects both the ruling family (Al Saud) and Islamic identity. September 23 is celebrated as Saudi National Day.

events.title: Commercial Oil Discovered at Dammam Well No. 7
events.detail: On March 3, 1938, Dammam Well No. 7 — nicknamed 'Prosperity Well' — strikes commercial oil for the California-Arabian Standard Oil Company (CASOC). The discovery transforms the Kingdom's future: Saudi Arabia would eventually become the world's largest oil exporter. By 1944, CASOC was renamed the Arabian American Oil Company (Aramco).

events.title: FDR Meets Ibn Saud aboard USS Quincy
events.detail: On February 14, 1945, President Franklin D. Roosevelt meets King Abd al-Aziz ibn Saud aboard the USS Quincy in the Great Bitter Lake of the Suez Canal. The meeting establishes what becomes the 'oil-for-security' framework: US access to Saudi oil in exchange for American security guarantees to the Kingdom. FDR's appeal for a Jewish state in Palestine met firm Saudi refusal.

events.title: Ibn Saud Dies; King Saud Assumes the Throne
events.detail: Abdulaziz ibn Saud, founder of modern Saudi Arabia, dies on November 9, 1953. He fathered at least 45 sons who would dominate Saudi politics for the next century. His eldest surviving son, Saud ibn Abdulaziz, assumes the throne, but his reign proves fiscally profligate, leading to a showdown with his brother Faisal who eventually deposes him in 1964.

events.title: OPEC Founded in Baghdad
events.detail: On September 14, 1960, Saudi Arabia joins Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, and Venezuela to found the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) in Baghdad. The trigger was major oil companies unilaterally cutting the 'posted price' of oil in August 1960. OPEC would eventually control price and production of the world's dominant energy source.

events.title: King Faisal Deposes Profligate King Saud
events.detail: In November 1964, senior princes and religious scholars declare King Saud unfit to rule due to fiscal mismanagement and debt. Crown Prince Faisal ibn Abdulaziz assumes the throne and launches a transformative modernization program: Saudi Arabia's first five-year plan, expansion of girls' education (over fierce religious opposition), introduction of television, and beginning of Aramco nationalization.

events.title: OAPEC Oil Embargo Shocks the West
events.detail: On October 17, 1973, Saudi Arabia leads OAPEC in an oil embargo against the US, Western Europe, and Japan for supporting Israel in the Yom Kippur War. Oil prices quadruple from ~$3 to ~$12 per barrel. Saudi revenues explode 40x between 1965 and 1975. The embargo creates long fuel lines across America and fundamentally reshapes global energy geopolitics.

events.title: Saudi Arabia Begins Nationalizing Saudi Aramco
events.detail: Saudi Arabia negotiates a 25% stake in Aramco with American oil companies in January 1973, increasing to 60% in 1974. Full nationalization is completed on November 13, 1988 when Saudi Aramco is officially established under full Saudi government ownership. The process transforms Saudi Arabia from a host-country into the owner-operator of the world's most valuable oil company.

events.title: King Faisal Assassinated by Nephew
events.detail: On March 25, 1975, King Faisal ibn Abdulaziz is shot twice in the head during a royal audience by his nephew Prince Faisal bin Musaid. The assassin is publicly beheaded on June 18, 1975. Faisal's modernizing legacy — expanding education, launching Saudi infrastructure, wielding the oil weapon — ended abruptly. He was succeeded by his half-brother Khalid.

events.title: Iranian Revolution Threatens Saudi Monarchy's Legitimacy
events.detail: Ayatollah Khomeini's Islamic Revolution overthrows the Shah of Iran in February 1979, establishing an Islamic Republic hostile to the Al Saud monarchy. Saudi Arabia and Iran become bitter regional rivals. Riyadh funds Saddam Hussein's Iraq in the Iran-Iraq War (1980–1988). The Revolutionary Guard explicitly targets the legitimacy of Al Saud's custodianship of Mecca and Medina, a claim that threatens the Kingdom's core religious identity.

events.title: Juhayman al-Otaybi Seizes the Grand Mosque in Mecca
events.detail: On November 20, 1979, Juhayman al-Otaybi leads 200–500 armed followers in seizing the Grand Mosque in Mecca, taking 50,000 worshippers hostage. He declared his brother-in-law Mohammed al-Qahtani as the Mahdi. Saudi forces, lacking capability for urban mosque combat, secretly employed French GIGN commandos (who briefly converted to Islam) to help retake the mosque after two weeks. 68 surviving insurgents were publicly beheaded on January 9, 1980.

events.title: Gulf War: Saudi Arabia Invites US Forces onto Holy Land
events.detail: After Iraq's August 2, 1990 invasion of Kuwait, King Fahd invites US and coalition troops to Saudi Arabia for Operation Desert Shield. Over 500,000 coalition troops deploy from Saudi soil. Operation Desert Storm (January–February 1991) expels Iraq from Kuwait. The presence of 'infidel' troops near the holy cities enrages Osama bin Laden and directly motivates his later jihad against both the US and the Saudi government.

events.title: Saudi Arabia Strips Bin Laden of Citizenship
events.detail: In April 1994, the Saudi government formally strips Osama bin Laden of citizenship and freezes his assets in the Kingdom, responding to his open calls for jihad against the Saudi royal family. Bin Laden had been based in Sudan since 1991 after being expelled from Saudi Arabia. The move did not stop al-Qaeda's growth: many wealthy Saudis continued to fund the network through charitable channels.

events.title: Khobar Towers Bombing Kills 19 US Troops
events.detail: On June 25, 1996, a massive truck bomb explodes outside the Khobar Towers military housing complex near Dhahran, killing 19 US Air Force personnel and wounding 372. The FBI investigation implicated Saudi Hezbollah, believed directed by Iran. The attack demonstrated the vulnerability of US forces in Saudi Arabia and led to major security relocations.

events.title: 9/11: 15 of 19 Hijackers Were Saudi Citizens
events.detail: The September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks kill approximately 3,000 people. Fifteen of the 19 hijackers were Saudi nationals. Saudi-born Osama bin Laden masterminded the attacks from Afghanistan. The attacks severely strain US-Saudi relations; later declassified FBI documents ('The 28 Pages') suggest Saudi government-linked individuals may have provided logistical support to some hijackers. Saudi Arabia denied state involvement.

media.source: Wikipedia / Hijackers in the September 11 attacks

events.title: Al-Qaeda Bombs Riyadh Residential Compounds
events.detail: On May 12, 2003, al-Qaeda operatives detonate three simultaneous car bombs at Western residential compounds in Riyadh, killing 35 and wounding ~200. The attacks galvanize the Saudi government into a major domestic counterterrorism campaign. The Interior Ministry arrests 600+ suspects and dismantles several al-Qaeda cells in the following months, effectively crushing al-Qaeda's operational presence inside the Kingdom.

events.title: King Abdullah Formally Assumes the Throne
events.detail: King Fahd bin Abdulaziz dies on August 1, 2005. Abdullah bin Abdulaziz, who had been de facto ruler since Fahd's 1995 stroke, formally becomes the 6th King. Abdullah pursues cautious reforms: limited women's economic participation, founding of KAUST (2009), and Saudi Arabia's first municipal elections for men (2005). In 2015, women were permitted to vote in municipal elections for the first time.

events.title: Saudi Arabia Suppresses Arab Spring; Deploys Troops to Bahrain
events.detail: As Arab Spring protests spread in 2011, Saudi Arabia launches a $130 billion social spending package to preempt domestic unrest. On March 14, 2011, Saudi Arabia deploys ~1,200 troops under the GCC Peninsula Shield Force to Bahrain to help crush Shia-led pro-democracy protests. This was the first use of Peninsula Shield for internal GCC suppression, drawing sharp international criticism.

events.title: King Salman Ascends; MBS Elevated to Defense Minister
events.detail: King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz dies on January 23, 2015. His half-brother Salman bin Abdulaziz becomes the 7th King. Salman immediately elevates his son Mohammed bin Salman (MBS) to Defense Minister and Second Deputy Crown Prince. Within two years, MBS would displace his cousin Mohammed bin Nayef as Crown Prince and become the Kingdom's de facto ruler.

events.title: Saudi Arabia Launches Operation Decisive Storm in Yemen
events.detail: On March 26, 2015, Saudi Arabia leads a nine-country coalition in bombing Houthi positions in Yemen after Houthis seized the capital Sanaa and forced President Hadi to flee. The campaign, supported by US intelligence and logistics, was intended to last weeks but extended for years. The UN estimates 233,000 total deaths by 2020, mostly from disease and famine caused by the conflict.

media.source: Wikipedia / Saudi-led intervention in the Yemeni civil war

events.title: MBS Unveils Vision 2030 Economic Transformation Plan
events.detail: On April 25, 2016, Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman publicly unveils Saudi Vision 2030, an ambitious plan targeting diversification away from oil dependence. Key targets: non-oil revenue increase from SR163 billion to SR1 trillion, FDI at 5.7% of GDP, unemployment reduced to 7%, female workforce participation raised from 17% to 30%, and 100 million annual tourists. The plan includes partial privatization of Saudi Aramco.

events.title: MBS Replaces MBN as Crown Prince; Succession Redrawn
events.detail: On June 21, 2017, King Salman replaces Crown Prince Mohammed bin Nayef (MBN) with his own son Mohammed bin Salman (MBS). MBN was reportedly detained and pressured to sign a resignation letter while sleep-deprived. MBN's removal ended the traditional succession among Ibn Saud's sons and established direct father-to-son succession — a historic shift in how Saudi power is transferred.

events.title: Saudi Arabia Leads Qatar Blockade
events.detail: On June 5, 2017, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Egypt, and Bahrain sever diplomatic relations and impose a land/sea/air blockade on Qatar, presenting 13 demands including closure of Al Jazeera and severance of ties with Iran. Qatar refused all demands. The blockade lasted 3.5 years, ending with the Al-Ula Declaration on January 5, 2021. The crisis exposed deep fractures within the Gulf Cooperation Council.

events.title: Anti-Corruption Purge: Ritz-Carlton Mass Arrests
events.detail: On November 4, 2017, over 200 Saudi princes, ministers, and businessmen are detained at the Ritz-Carlton hotel in Riyadh in what MBS called an anti-corruption campaign. The Saudi government announced $107 billion in recovered assets. Critics and human rights organizations described it as an extrajudicial power consolidation; detainees reportedly faced coercion and physical pressure before signing over assets without judicial oversight.

events.title: Saudi Arabia Lifts the Ban on Women Driving
events.detail: On June 24, 2018, Saudi Arabia lifts the world's only remaining ban on women driving. Ironically, several of the most prominent women's rights activists who had campaigned for this right were arrested weeks before the announcement and detained for months, with some reporting torture in detention. They were subsequently tried before the Specialized Criminal Court on terrorism-related charges.

events.title: Washington Post Journalist Jamal Khashoggi Murdered at Istanbul Consulate
events.detail: On October 2, 2018, Washington Post columnist Jamal Khashoggi enters the Saudi consulate in Istanbul to obtain marriage documents and is murdered by a 15-member Saudi operative team. Turkey shares audio recordings with multiple intelligence agencies. In November 2018, the CIA concludes that Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman personally ordered the assassination — a finding Saudi Arabia denies. Saudi courts sentenced five operatives to 20 years, three others to shorter terms.

events.title: Saudi Aramco Completes World's Largest IPO
events.detail: On December 11, 2019, Saudi Aramco lists on the Saudi Tadawul exchange, raising $25.6 billion — the world's largest IPO at the time. Aramco's valuation reached ~$1.7–2 trillion at listing. The IPO was a Vision 2030 cornerstone but relied heavily on domestic and Gulf investors after international interest fell short of MBS's ambitions.

events.title: Drone Attacks Cripple Abqaiq Oil Facility
events.detail: On September 14, 2019, simultaneous drone and cruise missile attacks strike Aramco's Abqaiq oil processing facility and Khurais oil field, disrupting 5.7 million barrels per day — half Saudi production and ~5% of global supply. The Houthis claimed credit, but the US, UK, France, Germany, and Saudi Arabia blamed Iran. Production was restored within weeks, but the attack exposed catastrophic vulnerability in Saudi energy infrastructure.

events.title: China Brokers Saudi-Iran Diplomatic Restoration
events.detail: On March 10, 2023, Saudi Arabia and Iran sign a China-brokered agreement in Beijing to restore full diplomatic relations, suspended since January 2016. Saudi Arabia reopened its Tehran embassy August 9, 2023; Iran reopened its Riyadh embassy June 6, 2023. The deal marked a major Chinese diplomatic achievement and signaled a new phase of Middle East multi-polarity, reducing US influence over regional diplomacy.

events.title: Vision 2030 Midterm: Gains Claimed, NEOM Scaled Back
events.detail: Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030 Annual Report 2024 reports 93% of interim indicators achieved: non-oil GDP at 55%, 122 million tourists, $913 billion in PIF assets. However, NEOM's 'The Line' construction was suspended in September 2025 with only 5 km completion planned by 2030 (down from 170 km). FDI of $31.7 billion in 2024 remains far below the $100 billion annual target. Carnegie Endowment notes 'significant progress but limited accountability' in reporting.
